parts.title: The Coded Message
parts.text: Thiago found a dusty notebook tucked behind a shelf in the school library. Someone had left an **annotation** beside every paragraph — tiny notes written in faded ink. Astrid leaned closer and tried to **articulate** what the markings meant, but the words were strange. Jaya grabbed the **glossary** at the back of the notebook, hoping it would explain the unusual terms.

parts.title: Cracking the Clues
parts.text: The three friends began a careful **discourse** about the mysterious text. Jaya noticed that one phrase was **figurative**, comparing the night sky to a blanket of secrets. Astrid studied the **etymology** of an old word, tracing its roots back to ancient Greek. Thiago tried to **paraphrase** a coded line, restating it in simpler language.

parts.title: The Hidden Meaning
parts.text: Some sentences held **ambiguity** — they could mean two completely different things. Astrid realized that a word's **connotation** mattered more than its dictionary definition in this puzzle. She asked Thiago to **enunciate** each clue aloud, pronouncing every syllable clearly. Jaya made an **inference** from the patterns she noticed. The writer had been far too **verbose**, using many extra words to hide a simple secret.

parts.title: The Reveal
parts.text: After hours of teamwork, the three friends decoded the final line. The notebook had been written decades ago by a student who wanted future readers to practice their language skills. They laughed, realizing the mystery had been a vocabulary exercise all along, and decided to create their own coded notebook for the next curious reader.

definitions.meaning1: To express an idea clearly and effectively. Example: She was able to articulate her feelings about the book during the class discussion.
definitions.meaning2: Able to speak clearly and well. Example: The articulate speaker held the audience's attention the entire time.
definitions.studentChallenge: He tried to ___ his thoughts, but he was too nervous to find the right words.

definitions.meaning1: The study of where words come from and how their meanings have changed over time. Example: The etymology of the word 'telephone' traces back to Greek words meaning 'far' and 'voice.'
definitions.studentChallenge: Learning the ___ of a word can help you understand its meaning better.

definitions.meaning1: To restate something in your own words. Example: The teacher asked us to paraphrase the paragraph instead of copying it word for word.
definitions.meaning2: A restatement of a text in different words. Example: Her paraphrase of the poem made it much easier to understand.
definitions.studentChallenge: Can you ___ this sentence so it is easier for younger students to understand?

definitions.meaning1: When something can be understood in more than one way; uncertainty of meaning. Example: The ambiguity of the riddle made it hard to know the correct answer.
definitions.studentChallenge: There was ___ in his directions, so we were not sure which path to take.

definitions.meaning1: An extra feeling or idea that a word suggests beyond its basic definition. Example: The word 'home' has a warm connotation that the word 'building' does not.
definitions.studentChallenge: The word 'stubborn' has a negative ___, while 'determined' sounds more positive.

definitions.meaning1: Using words in a creative, non-literal way to create a picture or idea. Example: When she said 'it's raining cats and dogs,' she was using figurative language.
definitions.studentChallenge: The poet used ___ language when she called the moon a silver coin in the sky.

definitions.meaning1: A list of special or unusual words with their definitions, usually found at the back of a book. Example: I checked the glossary to find the meaning of the science term.
definitions.studentChallenge: If you do not know a word in the textbook, look it up in the ___ at the back.

definitions.meaning1: A note or comment added to a text to explain or highlight something. Example: The student wrote an annotation in the margin to remember the important detail.
definitions.studentChallenge: She added an ___ next to the poem to remind herself what the metaphor meant.

definitions.meaning1: Using more words than necessary; long-winded. Example: His verbose essay could have been half the length if he had cut the extra details.
definitions.studentChallenge: The report was too ___, so the teacher asked her to shorten it.

definitions.meaning1: A serious discussion or conversation about a topic. Example: The class had an interesting discourse about whether homework helps students learn.
definitions.studentChallenge: The students had a thoughtful ___ about the causes of the American Revolution.

definitions.meaning1: A conclusion you reach based on clues and evidence, not from being told directly. Example: Based on the wet umbrella by the door, she made the inference that it was raining outside.
definitions.studentChallenge: The detective made an ___ about who stole the painting based on the footprints.

definitions.meaning1: To pronounce words clearly and distinctly. Example: The actor had to enunciate every word so the audience in the back row could understand.
definitions.studentChallenge: Please ___ your words when giving a speech so everyone can hear you clearly.

doubleTakeQuiz.sentence1: She was able to _______ her ideas clearly during the group presentation.
doubleTakeQuiz.sentence2: The _______ young speaker impressed the judges with how well she expressed her thoughts.

doubleTakeQuiz.sentence1: The _______ of the word 'democracy' traces back to ancient Greek words meaning 'people' and 'rule.'
doubleTakeQuiz.sentence2: Studying the _______ of English words reveals that many come from Latin and French roots.

doubleTakeQuiz.sentence1: The teacher asked us to _______ the author's main argument using our own words.
doubleTakeQuiz.sentence2: If you cannot quote the text exactly, try to _______ the most important ideas instead.

doubleTakeQuiz.sentence1: The _______ of the poem's ending left readers debating what really happened to the character.
doubleTakeQuiz.sentence2: To avoid _______, the scientist wrote her instructions in very specific, clear language.

doubleTakeQuiz.sentence1: The word 'cheap' has a negative _______, while 'affordable' sounds much more positive.
doubleTakeQuiz.sentence2: Writers choose words carefully because every word carries a _______ beyond its basic meaning.

doubleTakeQuiz.sentence1: Saying 'time flies' is _______ language because time does not actually have wings.
doubleTakeQuiz.sentence2: The poet used _______ expressions to compare sadness to a heavy stone on the chest.

doubleTakeQuiz.sentence1: I found the definition of 'photosynthesis' in the _______ at the back of the science textbook.
doubleTakeQuiz.sentence2: The _______ listed every technical term used in the chapter in alphabetical order.

doubleTakeQuiz.sentence1: The student wrote an _______ in the margin of the page explaining why the quote was important.
doubleTakeQuiz.sentence2: Each _______ she added to the map helped the other hikers understand the trail better.

doubleTakeQuiz.sentence1: His _______ answer took ten minutes when a simple 'yes' or 'no' would have been enough.
doubleTakeQuiz.sentence2: The _______ letter rambled on for five pages without ever getting to the point.

doubleTakeQuiz.sentence1: The panel held a thoughtful _______ about how technology is changing education.
doubleTakeQuiz.sentence2: During their _______, the students shared different viewpoints about the novel's theme.

doubleTakeQuiz.sentence1: Based on the dark clouds and strong wind, she made the _______ that a storm was coming.
doubleTakeQuiz.sentence2: Good readers make an _______ about a character's feelings by looking at what the character says and does.

doubleTakeQuiz.sentence1: The drama teacher reminded the actors to _______ every syllable so the audience could understand them.
doubleTakeQuiz.sentence2: She had to _______ the unfamiliar name several times before her classmates could repeat it correctly.

secretPassage.instructions: Read the passage below. Fill in each blank with the correct vocabulary word from the word bank.
secretPassage.passage:
Professor Lin's Language Lab was the most popular after-school club at Maple Ridge Elementary. Every Tuesday, students gathered for a lively _(1)_ about the hidden secrets of words. Today's lesson began with the _(2)_ of the word 'astronaut,' which comes from Greek words meaning 'star sailor' — a beautiful _(3)_ expression that paints a picture of someone sailing through the sky.

Marcus raised his hand and tried to _(4)_ his question clearly, but he stumbled over the long word 'onomatopoeia.' Professor Lin smiled and asked him to _(5)_ each syllable slowly. She then pointed to the _(6)_ in their workbook where the word was defined.

Next, Professor Lin showed two poems on the board. She had written an _(7)_ beside each stanza explaining the poet's technique. She asked the class to _(8)_ the first poem's meaning in their own words. One student's answer was far too _(9)_, taking three paragraphs to say what could be said in three sentences.

The trickiest part came when Professor Lin revealed a sentence with deliberate _(10)_ — it could be read two different ways. The students had to make an _(11)_ about the author's true intention using context clues. Finally, they discussed how the word 'thrifty' has a positive _(12)_ compared to the word 'stingy,' even though both describe someone who does not spend much money.

wordScaleImposter.studentPick: Pick 2 imposters and explain why they are opposites of the vocabulary word.
